title: Air pollution from burning fuels
examTip: AQA tests which pollutant comes from which source, and what harm each causes. The key four are: CO₂ (greenhouse gas), CO (toxic), SO₂ (acid rain), NOₓ (acid rain + smog). Carbon particles (soot/particulates) also appear. Know both the formation and the effects for each.
introTips: Every pollutant has a specific source and specific effect — don't mix them up. CO₂ causes climate change; CO is toxic; SO₂ and NOₓ cause acid rain.
Sulfur in fuel → SO₂. Nitrogen from air reacts at high temperatures in engines → NOₓ.
AQA sometimes asks how to reduce pollution — catalytic converters for vehicles, and removing sulfur from fuels before burning.
intro: Burning fuels in vehicles, power stations, and industry releases a mixture of gases and particles into the atmosphere. Some are greenhouse gases that drive climate change. Others are directly toxic or cause acid rain that damages ecosystems. Understanding what's produced, why, and what harm it causes — and what we can do about it — is the full picture AQA expects.

keyPoints.heading: Carbon dioxide and carbon monoxide
keyPoints.content: Complete combustion of fossil fuels produces CO₂ and water. CO₂ is a greenhouse gas — its increasing concentration is the primary driver of climate change (see c9-greenhouse). Incomplete combustion occurs when there is insufficient oxygen. This produces carbon monoxide (CO) and/or solid carbon particles (soot/particulates). Carbon monoxide is a colourless, odourless, highly toxic gas — it binds to haemoglobin in red blood cells in place of oxygen, preventing oxygen transport. Even small concentrations can be fatal. Carbon particles (soot) cause respiratory problems and carry toxic compounds into the lungs. They also contribute to smog in cities.
writeBullets: Complete combustion: fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O
Incomplete combustion (insufficient O₂): produces CO (carbon monoxide) and/or soot (carbon particles)
CO is colourless, odourless, toxic — binds to haemoglobin, prevents O₂ transport
Soot (particulates): respiratory problems, contributes to smog
CO₂: greenhouse gas — drives climate change

keyTerms.def: Burning with sufficient oxygen — produces CO₂ and water only
Burning with insufficient oxygen — produces CO (carbon monoxide) and/or soot (carbon particles)
A toxic colourless gas produced by incomplete combustion; binds to haemoglobin and prevents oxygen transport in the blood

keyPoints.memTip: Complete = CO₂ + H₂O (clean, but contributes to greenhouse effect). Incomplete = CO + soot (toxic and smoggy). The difference is oxygen supply. A blocked flue or poorly maintained boiler → incomplete combustion → CO poisoning risk.

keyPoints.heading: Sulfur dioxide — acid rain
keyPoints.content: Many fossil fuels (especially coal and crude oil) contain sulfur impurities. When these fuels burn, the sulfur reacts with oxygen to form sulfur dioxide (SO₂). SO₂ dissolves in rainwater to form sulfurous acid, contributing to acid rain (pH typically 4–5 rather than normal 5.6). Effects of acid rain: damages leaves and roots of trees; acidifies lakes and rivers (kills fish and aquatic organisms); corrodes limestone and marble buildings and statues; damages metal structures. Solutions: remove sulfur from fuels before burning (desulfurisation); use low-sulfur fuels; fit scrubbers to power station chimneys to remove SO₂ from flue gases.
writeBullets: Sulfur in fossil fuels → burns → SO₂ produced
SO₂ + rainwater → sulfurous acid → acid rain
Acid rain effects: harms trees, kills aquatic organisms, corrodes limestone buildings
Solution: remove sulfur from fuel (desulfurisation), low-sulfur fuels, flue gas scrubbers

keyTerms.def: A gas produced when fuels containing sulfur impurities are burned — dissolves in rain to form acid rain
Rain with a pH lower than normal (below 5.6) due to dissolved SO₂ and NOₓ — damages ecosystems, buildings, and metals
The removal of sulfur from fossil fuels before they are burned, to reduce SO₂ emissions

keyPoints.memTip: Sulfur in fuel → SO₂ → acid rain. It's a simple chain. The key distinction: SO₂ comes from sulfur impurities in fuel. NOₓ comes from nitrogen in the air reacting at high temperature. Different source, similar effect (both contribute to acid rain).

keyPoints.heading: Nitrogen oxides — another acid rain contributor
keyPoints.content: At the high temperatures inside combustion engines, nitrogen and oxygen from the air react together to form nitrogen oxides (NOₓ) — mainly NO and NO₂. This doesn't come from the fuel — it comes from the air itself. NOₓ dissolves in rainwater to form nitric acid, contributing to acid rain. NOₓ also reacts in sunlight with unburned hydrocarbons to form photochemical smog — a haze of ozone and other irritants that affects air quality in cities and causes respiratory problems. Catalytic converters in vehicle exhaust systems convert CO and NOₓ into less harmful CO₂ and N₂.
writeBullets: NOₓ formed when N₂ from air reacts with O₂ at high combustion temperatures
NOₓ source = high-temperature combustion (engines), NOT sulfur impurities in fuel
NOₓ + rainwater → nitric acid → acid rain
NOₓ + sunlight + unburned hydrocarbons → photochemical smog
Catalytic converters: convert CO and NOₓ → CO₂ + N₂

keyTerms.def: Gases formed when atmospheric nitrogen and oxygen react at high temperatures in combustion engines — contribute to acid rain and smog
A type of air pollution formed when NOₓ and unburned hydrocarbons react in sunlight, producing ozone and other irritants
A device fitted to vehicle exhausts that uses a catalyst to convert harmful CO and NOₓ into less harmful CO₂ and N₂

keyPoints.memTip: NOₓ = N from AIR (not fuel!) + O, at high temperature. Sulfur comes from FUEL. Both contribute to acid rain. Catalytic converter converts: CO → CO₂ and NOₓ → N₂. The converter is the vehicle solution; desulfurisation is the power station solution for SO₂.

tables.title: Air pollutants from burning fuels
rows: Toxic — binds to haemoglobin, prevents O₂ transport
Ensure adequate oxygen supply; catalytic converters
Acid rain → harms trees, fish, buildings

0.question: Where does the sulfur dioxide produced during combustion come from?
options: Nitrogen in the air reacting with oxygen at high temperatures
Sulfur impurities in fossil fuels reacting with oxygen when burned
Incomplete combustion of hydrocarbons when oxygen is limited
The reaction of CO₂ with rainwater to form an acid
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — sulfur impurities in fuels are the source. When sulfur burns, it forms SO₂. The sulfur content of fuels varies — coal tends to have more sulfur than natural gas.
0.wrongFeedback: SO₂ comes from sulfur impurities that are naturally present in many fossil fuels (especially coal). When the fuel burns, the sulfur reacts with oxygen to form SO₂. Nitrogen reacting with oxygen at high temperature produces NOₓ — a different pollutant with similar effects.

1.question: Why is carbon monoxide particularly dangerous?
options: It is explosive and ignites easily in the presence of oxygen
It is colourless and odourless, and binds to haemoglobin preventing oxygen transport
It reacts with rainwater to form a strong acid that harms ecosystems
It absorbs infrared radiation and acts as a powerful greenhouse gas
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — CO's danger lies in being undetectable by senses AND having a much higher affinity for haemoglobin than oxygen does. It effectively suffocates from the inside.
1.wrongFeedback: Carbon monoxide is dangerous because it is colourless and odourless (you can't detect it), and it binds to haemoglobin in red blood cells far more strongly than oxygen does. This means red blood cells can't carry oxygen, causing tissue suffocation. CO detectors are essential in homes with gas boilers or open fires.

commonMistakes: Saying NOₓ comes from nitrogen in the fuel — most fuels don't contain significant nitrogen. NOₓ forms because nitrogen and oxygen from the AIR react together at the high temperatures inside combustion engines.
Saying CO₂ is directly toxic — it's not (in normal concentrations). Its harm is indirect, through the greenhouse effect. CO (carbon monoxide) is directly toxic.
Confusing SO₂ and CO effects — SO₂ → acid rain → harms ecosystems and buildings. CO → toxic gas → prevents oxygen transport in blood. Two very different problems.
Forgetting that catalytic converters convert CO to CO₂ — CO₂ is less harmful than CO, but it's still a greenhouse gas. The converter doesn't eliminate pollution entirely.
examTips: For each pollutant, learn: (1) what makes it (source), (2) what harm it causes (effect), (3) how to reduce it (solution). AQA tests all three parts.
When asked about acid rain causes, name BOTH SO₂ and NOₓ — one answer rarely gets full marks for a 2-mark question.
Distinguish CO from CO₂ carefully — one letter makes a huge difference. CO = toxic gas (incomplete combustion). CO₂ = greenhouse gas (complete combustion).
Catalytic converter questions: it converts CO and NOₓ into CO₂ and N₂. State both inputs AND both outputs.
revisionCardBullets: Complete combustion: fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O
Incomplete combustion: insufficient O₂ → CO (toxic) + soot
CO: colourless, odourless, toxic — binds to haemoglobin, blocks O₂ transport
SO₂: from sulfur impurities in fossil fuels → dissolves in rain → acid rain
Acid rain: damages trees, kills aquatic organisms, corrodes limestone
NOₓ: N₂ from air + O₂ at high temperatures in engines → acid rain + photochemical smog
Catalytic converter: converts CO + NOₓ → CO₂ + N₂
Desulfurisation: removes sulfur from fuel before burning → reduces SO₂
Soot/particulates: respiratory damage, contributes to urban smog
